title: The Roc
summary: There are birds, and then there is the Roc. Centers on Roc,  from Saudi Arabia mythology. Known for nature manipulation and animal communication and wind control..
full_text:
There are birds, and then there is the Roc. In all the tales of travel and wonder that come from the East, from the Thousand Nights and One, there is no creature to match it—a bird so vast that its wings could eclipse the sun, that its shadow could cover an entire fleet of ships, that it could carry an elephant as easily as an eagle carries a mouse.

The Roc makes its nest in the Mountains of the Moon—a range spoken of only in stories, somewhere beyond the edges of maps. Here, the Roc builds its nests on the highest peaks, from branches of trees as thick as a man's body. Its eggs are spoken of only in wonder: so large that a single egg would feed a hundred men, so hard they needed special tools to crack.

The great sailor Sinbad—famous in the Arabian tales for his voyages—encountered the Roc on his second journey. He and his crew were shipwrecked on an island, and when morning came, he found himself being lifted into the air. Above him, great wings beat the sky. The Roc had mistaken his boat—the ship—for some new kind of creature, or perhaps for prey.

Sinbad knew that he faced death from a great height. He was carried, helpless, for immeasurable distances, above clouds and countries. But he was clever—he unbound himself from his supposed captor, letting himself fall. He landed, battered and broken in spirit. But he lived to tell the tale.

This is why we imagine giant birds—not in the small birds we see, but in creatures of scale beyond our knowing. And why we remember the Roc as greatest—because it is the measure of all things: size, distance, the unknown. Those who hear its story learn that there are wonders in this world larger than we can comprehend.

title: Djinn
summary: Before humans, before the world was made as it is now, there were beings made of smokeless fire—beings that did not burn, because they were the fire itself.
full_text:
Before humans, before the world was made as it is now, there were beings made of smokeless fire—beings that did not burn, because they were the fire itself. These are the Djinn, creatures of smoke and flame and spirit, made from fire without smoke, as humans were made from earth. They have existed since before memory, since when the world was young.

Unlike angels, who do not question and cannot disobey, the Djinn have free will. Some turned to good; some turned to evil—as varied as humans, for they have souls and choices. They live in a world parallel to ours, unseen but present, in ruins and mountains, in the spaces between things. They can help or hinder, depending on their nature.

In the Quran—the holy book—they are mentioned, mentioned as beings with souls, as creatures that must be answered for. This gave them a place in the faith, not as gods, but as beings with moral weight. They are not to be worshipped, but they are not to be ignored.

King Solomon, the wise, the blessed, controlled them. He had a ring that carried the name of God—the most powerful name, the name that gives power over all things. With it, he commanded the Djinn, making them build his temple, his great house of worship in Jerusalem. They lifted stones and placed them, though they resented working for mortals.

But there are tales beyond this. The fisherman who opened a vessel and freed something old, something patient—which had been waiting for centuries. TheIfrit, the powerful djinn of underground fire. The stories change, but each warns: be careful with spirits. They are real; they have power; they are not always kind.

This is why we remember: djinn exist, in story and in worldview. And why we are careful with what we open—the ring in the story was not opened wisely.

title: Ifrit
summary: The powerful djinn of the underground. Centers on Ifrit,  from Arabia. jinn associated with ruin..
full_text:
Among the Djinn, there are ranks. There are the Marid, the great ones of the sea, vast and wild. There are theJinn, common spirits, as varied as humans in their nature. And there are the Ifrit—the most powerful, the most dangerous, the most terrible of all.

The Ifrit are djinn of the underground—born from fire, shaped from smoke and earth, dwelling in places beneath the earth where the heat rises and no light penetrates. They are not demons, not in the Christian sense, but they are dangerous. Where other djinn can be bargained with, the Ifrit are proud—too proud to serve without cost, too powerful to control without force.

They can grant wishes—but the wishes come at a price. A mortal might ask for wealth, and wealth they will receive—but in ways that bring tragedy: the death of loved ones, the loss of health, the slow corruption of everything they touched. The Ifrit do not care about human happiness. They see mortals as toys, as things to use.

Even Solomon, who controlled djinn with the name of God, had to work harder for the Ifrit. One famous tale tells of an Ifrit bound to build a gate for the temple—a task so heavy that even a djinn would struggle. Solomon tricked it, using a ring with the sacred name, and it built—but it was furious, and swore revenge that never came because Solomon was wise.

This is why we fear the underground: places of hidden fire. And why we are careful with powerful spirits—some are beyond bargaining, beyond safety, beyond anything but respect and avoidance.

The Ifrit still live, the stories say, in places deep below. They wait, they plot, they grow in power. To encounter one is to court disaster.
